### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Lithuania](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Lithuania "Foreign relations of Lithuania")

Lithuania became a member of the United Nations on 18 September 1991, and is a signatory to a number of its organizations and other international agreements. It is also a member of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"), [Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Cooperation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe"), as well as [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") and its adjunct North Atlantic Coordinating Council. Lithuania gained membership in the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") on 31 May 2001, and joined the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") on 5 July 2018,[[147]](#cite_note-Lithuania's_accession_to_the_OECD-151) while also seeking membership in other Western organizations. Lithuania has established diplomatic relations with 149 countries.[[148]](#cite_note-152) During the second half of 2013, Lithuania assumed the role of the [presidency of the European Union](/wiki/Presidency_of_the_Council_of_the_European_Union "Presidency of the Council of the European Union"). Lithuania is active in developing cooperation among northern European countries. It is a member of the interparliamentary [Baltic Assembly](/wiki/Baltic_Assembly "Baltic Assembly"), the intergovernmental [Baltic Council of Ministers](/wiki/Baltic_Council_of_Ministers "Baltic Council of Ministers") and the [Council of the Baltic Sea States](/wiki/Council_of_the_Baltic_Sea_States "Council of the Baltic Sea States").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Lithuania_presidency_EU_stamp_2013.jpg/250px-Lithuania_presidency_EU_stamp_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lithuania_presidency_EU_stamp_2013.jpg)

Stamp dedicated to Lithuania's presidency of the European Union

Lithuania cooperates with Nordic and the two other Baltic countries through the [Nordic-Baltic Eight](/wiki/Nordic-Baltic_Eight "Nordic-Baltic Eight") format. A similar format, NB6, unites Nordic and Baltic members of EU. NB6's focus is to discuss and agree on positions before presenting them to the Council of the European Union and at the meetings of EU foreign affairs ministers. The [Council of the Baltic Sea States](/wiki/Council_of_the_Baltic_Sea_States "Council of the Baltic Sea States") (CBSS) was established in [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen") in 1992 as an informal regional political forum. Its main aim is to promote integration and to close contacts between the region's countries. The members of CBSS are [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), Lithuania, [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), and the European Commission. Its observer states are [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine").

The [Nordic Council of Ministers](/wiki/Nordic_Council "Nordic Council") and Lithuania engage in political cooperation to attain mutual goals and to determine new trends and possibilities for joint cooperation. The council's information office aims to disseminate [Nordic](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") concepts and to demonstrate and promote Nordic cooperation. Together with the five Nordic countries and the two other Baltic countries, it is a member of the [Nordic Investment Bank (NIB)](/wiki/Nordic_Investment_Bank "Nordic Investment Bank") and cooperates in its NORDPLUS programme, which is committed to education.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Bachelet_en_Consejo_de_Seguridad_de_la_ONU_%282015%29.jpg/250px-Bachelet_en_Consejo_de_Seguridad_de_la_ONU_%282015%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bachelet_en_Consejo_de_Seguridad_de_la_ONU_(2015).jpg)

Lithuania was a member of the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council"). Its representatives are on the right side.

Poland was highly supportive of Lithuanian independence, despite the [discriminatory treatment](/wiki/Lithuanization "Lithuanization") of its [Polish minority](/wiki/Poles_in_Lithuania "Poles in Lithuania").[[149]](#cite_note-153)[[150]](#cite_note-154) The former [Solidarity](/wiki/Solidarity_(Polish_trade_union) "Solidarity (Polish trade union)") leader and Polish President [Lech Wałęsa](/wiki/Lech_Wa%C5%82%C4%99sa "Lech Wałęsa") criticised the government of Lithuania over discrimination against the Polish minority and rejected the [Order of Vytautas the Great](/wiki/Order_of_Vytautas_the_Great "Order of Vytautas the Great").[[151]](#cite_note-155) Lithuania maintains warm relations with [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)") and strongly supports its European Union and NATO aspirations.[[152]](#cite_note-156)[[153]](#cite_note-157)[[154]](#cite_note-158) During the [Russo-Georgian War](/wiki/Russo-Georgian_War "Russo-Georgian War") in 2008, when the Russian troops were occupying the territory of Georgia and approaching towards the Georgian capital [Tbilisi](/wiki/Tbilisi "Tbilisi"), President [Valdas Adamkus](/wiki/Valdas_Adamkus "Valdas Adamkus"), together with the Polish and Ukrainian presidents, went to Tbilisi by answering to the Georgians request of the international assistance.[[155]](#cite_note-159)[[156]](#cite_note-160) Shortly, Lithuanians and the [Lithuanian Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Lithuania "Catholic Church in Lithuania") also began collecting financial support for the war victims.[[157]](#cite_note-161)[[158]](#cite_note-162)

In 2004–2009, [Dalia Grybauskaitė](/wiki/Dalia_Grybauskait%C4%97 "Dalia Grybauskaitė") served as [European Commissioner for Financial Programming and the Budget](/wiki/European_Commissioner_for_Financial_Programming_and_the_Budget "European Commissioner for Financial Programming and the Budget") within the [José Manuel Barroso](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Manuel_Barroso "José Manuel Barroso")-led Commission.[[159]](#cite_note-163)[[160]](#cite_note-164)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Guests_of_the_2023_Vilnius_NATO_Summit_in_the_Courtyard_of_the_Presidential_Palace_in_Vilnius%2C_Lithuania.jpg/250px-Guests_of_the_2023_Vilnius_NATO_Summit_in_the_Courtyard_of_the_Presidential_Palace_in_Vilnius%2C_Lithuania.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guests_of_the_2023_Vilnius_NATO_Summit_in_the_Courtyard_of_the_Presidential_Palace_in_Vilnius,_Lithuania.jpg)

Guests of the 2023 Vilnius (NATO) summit in the Courtyard of the Presidential Palace in Vilnius

In 2013, Lithuania was elected to the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") for a two-year term,[[161]](#cite_note-165) becoming the first Baltic country elected to this post. During its membership, Lithuania actively supported [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") and often condemned Russia for the [war in Ukraine](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War"), immediately earning Ukrainian esteem.[[162]](#cite_note-166)[[163]](#cite_note-167) As the [war in Donbas](/wiki/War_in_Donbas "War in Donbas") progressed, President Dalia Grybauskaitė compared the Russian President [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") to [Josef Stalin](/wiki/Josef_Stalin "Josef Stalin") and to [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler"); she also called Russia a "terrorist state".[[164]](#cite_note-168)

In 2018 Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia were awarded the [Peace of Westphalia Prize](/w/index.php?title=Peace_of_Westphalia_Prize&action=edit&redlink=1 "Peace of Westphalia Prize (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Internationaler_Preis_des_Westf%C3%A4lischen_Friedens "de:Internationaler Preis des Westfälischen Friedens")] for their exceptional model of democratic development and contribution to peace in the continent.[[165]](#cite_note-169) In 2019 Lithuania condemned the [Turkish offensive into north-eastern Syria](/wiki/2019_Turkish_offensive_into_north-eastern_Syria "2019 Turkish offensive into north-eastern Syria").[[166]](#cite_note-170) In December 2021, Lithuania reported that in an escalation of the diplomatic spat with China over its [relations with Taiwan](/wiki/Lithuania%E2%80%93Taiwan_relations "Lithuania–Taiwan relations"),[[167]](#cite_note-171) China had stopped all imports from Lithuania.[[168]](#cite_note-172) According to Lithuanian intelligence agencies, in 2023 there was an increase in Chinese intelligence activity against Lithuania, including cyberespionage and increased focus on Lithuania's internal affairs and foreign policy.[[169]](#cite_note-173)

The 2023 [NATO summit](/wiki/NATO_summit "NATO summit") was held in Vilnius.[[170]](#cite_note-174)

### Military

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Closing_ceremony_for_Iron_Sword_2014.jpg/250px-Closing_ceremony_for_Iron_Sword_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Closing_ceremony_for_Iron_Sword_2014.jpg)

Lithuanian Army soldiers with their [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") allies during Iron Sword 2014

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Lithuanian_PzH_2000_unit_during_exercises.jpg/250px-Lithuanian_PzH_2000_unit_during_exercises.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lithuanian_PzH_2000_unit_during_exercises.jpg)

Lithuanian [Panzerhaubitze 2000](/wiki/Panzerhaubitze_2000 "Panzerhaubitze 2000") firing during an exercise in 2022

The [Lithuanian Armed Forces](/wiki/Lithuanian_Armed_Forces "Lithuanian Armed Forces") comprises the [Lithuanian Land Force](/wiki/Lithuanian_Land_Force "Lithuanian Land Force"), [Lithuanian Air Force](/wiki/Lithuanian_Air_Force "Lithuanian Air Force"), [Lithuanian Naval Force](/wiki/Lithuanian_Naval_Force "Lithuanian Naval Force"), [Lithuanian Special Operations Force](/wiki/Lithuanian_Special_Operations_Force "Lithuanian Special Operations Force") and other units: Logistics Command, Training and Doctrine Command, Headquarters Battalion, [Military Police](/wiki/Lithuanian_Military_Police "Lithuanian Military Police"). Directly subordinated to the [Chief of Defence](/wiki/Chief_of_Defence_(Lithuania) "Chief of Defence (Lithuania)") are the Special Operations Forces and Military Police. The Reserve Forces are under command of the [Lithuanian National Defence Volunteer Forces](/wiki/Lithuanian_National_Defence_Volunteer_Forces "Lithuanian National Defence Volunteer Forces").

The Lithuanian Armed Forces consist of some 20,000 active personnel, which may be supported by reserve forces.[[171]](#cite_note-175) Compulsory conscription ended in 2008 but was reintroduced in 2015.[[172]](#cite_note-176) As of 2024, the Lithuanian Armed Forces have 30 soldiers and officers participating in nine international operations and European Union training missions deployed in [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq"), [Central African Republic](/wiki/Central_African_Republic "Central African Republic"), [Djibouti](/wiki/Djibouti "Djibouti"), [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), and in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), providing training for Ukrainian soldiers on [Operation Interflex](/wiki/Operation_Interflex "Operation Interflex").[[173]](#cite_note-177)[[174]](#cite_note-178)

Lithuania became a full member of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") in March 2004. Fighter jets of NATO members are deployed in [Šiauliai Air Base](/wiki/%C5%A0iauliai_Air_Base "Šiauliai Air Base") and [provide security for the Baltic airspace](/wiki/Baltic_Air_Policing "Baltic Air Policing"). Since 2014, Lithuania participates in the British-led [Joint Expeditionary Force](/wiki/Joint_Expeditionary_Force "Joint Expeditionary Force").[[175]](#cite_note-179)

Beginning in summer of 2005, Lithuania was part of the [International Security Assistance Force](/wiki/International_Security_Assistance_Force "International Security Assistance Force") in Afghanistan, leading a [Provincial Reconstruction Team](/wiki/Provincial_Reconstruction_Team "Provincial Reconstruction Team") in [Chaghcharan](/wiki/Chaghcharan "Chaghcharan") in [Ghor Province](/wiki/Ghor_Province "Ghor Province"). Since joining international operations in 1994, Lithuania has lost two soldiers: Lieutenant Normundas Valteris fell in Bosnia, as his patrol vehicle drove over a mine. Sergeant Arūnas Jarmalavičius was fatally wounded during an attack on the camp of his Provincial Reconstruction Team in Afghanistan.[[176]](#cite_note-180)

The Lithuanian National Defence Policy aims to guarantee the preservation of the independence and sovereignty of the state, the integrity of its land, territorial waters and airspace, and its constitutional order. Its main strategic goals are to defend the country's interests, and to maintain and expand the capabilities of its armed forces so they may contribute to and participate in the missions of NATO and European Union member states.[[177]](#cite_note-WB-181)

The [defense ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_National_Defence_(Lithuania) "Ministry of National Defence (Lithuania)") is responsible for combat forces, search and rescue, and intelligence operations. The 5,000 [border guards](/wiki/Lithuanian_State_Border_Guard_Service "Lithuanian State Border Guard Service") fall under the Interior Ministry's supervision and are responsible for border protection, passport and customs duties, and share responsibility with the navy for smuggling and drug trafficking interdiction. A special security department handles VIP protection and communications security. In 2015 [National Cyber Security Centre of Lithuania](/wiki/National_Cyber_Security_Centre_of_Lithuania "National Cyber Security Centre of Lithuania") was created. Paramilitary organisation [Lithuanian Riflemen's Union](/wiki/Lithuanian_Riflemen%27s_Union "Lithuanian Riflemen's Union") acts as a civilian self-defence institution.

According to NATO, in 2020, Lithuania allocated 2.13% of its [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") to the national defense.[[178]](#cite_note-nato-lt-spending-182) For a long time, especially after the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), Lithuania lagged behind NATO allies in terms of defence spending. However, it increased funding, exceeding the NATO guideline of 2% in 2019. President Nausėda called for more NATO troops on 22 April 2022, saying NATO should increase its deployment of troops in Lithuania and elsewhere on Europe's eastern flank following the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), during a meeting in Vilnius.[[179]](#cite_note-183)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Lithuania](/wiki/Economy_of_Lithuania "Economy of Lithuania")

See also: [List of companies of Lithuania](/wiki/List_of_companies_of_Lithuania "List of companies of Lithuania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/GDP_per_capita_Baltics.svg/250px-GDP_per_capita_Baltics.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_Baltics.svg)

Real GDP per capita development of Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Lithuanian_counties_by_GDP_per_capita%2C_2022.png/250px-Lithuanian_counties_by_GDP_per_capita%2C_2022.png)](/wiki/File:Lithuanian_counties_by_GDP_per_capita,_2022.png)

Lithuanian counties by GDP per capita, 2022[[146]](#cite_note-Counties-150)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Lithuania%27s_GDP_per_capita_compared_to_rest_of_the_world_%282020%29.png/250px-Lithuania%27s_GDP_per_capita_compared_to_rest_of_the_world_%282020%29.png)](/wiki/File:Lithuania%27s_GDP_per_capita_compared_to_rest_of_the_world_(2020).png)

Comparison of Lithuania's GDP per capita to rest of the world where countries with higher GDP per capita are marked in red (2022)[[180]](#cite_note-184)

Lithuania has an open and [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy") that is classified as a [high-income economy](/wiki/High-income_economy "High-income economy") by the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank").[[181]](#cite_note-185) As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Lithuania&action=edit) the three largest sectors were services (63% of GDP), industry (24%) and agriculture (3%).[[182]](#cite_note-186) On 1 January 2015, the euro became the national currency, replacing [litas](/wiki/Lithuanian_litas "Lithuanian litas"), which had been in circulation since 1993.[[183]](#cite_note-iso4217news2014-187)

Mineral products comprised the largest share of exports (14%) in 2024; other major sectors include machinery and appliances, electrical equipment (14%), chemical products (11%), food, beverages and tobacco products (9%). The largest markets for exports were Latvia (12%), Poland (10%), and Germany (9%).[[184]](#cite_note-188) Exports equaled 88% of GDP in 2022.[[185]](#cite_note-189)

GDP experienced very high real growth rates for the decade up to 2009, peaking at 11% in 2007. As a result, the country was often termed a [Baltic Tiger](/wiki/Baltic_Tiger "Baltic Tiger"). However, in 2009 due to the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), GDP contracted 15%[[186]](#cite_note-190) and unemployment rate reached 17.8% in 2010.[[187]](#cite_note-191) Growth has since been much slower. According to the IMF, financial conditions are conducive to growth and financial soundness indicators remain strong. The public debt ratio in 2016 was 40% of GDP; it had been 15% in 2008.[[188]](#cite_note-192)

On average, more than 95% of all [foreign direct investment](/wiki/Foreign_direct_investment "Foreign direct investment") (FDI) comes from EU countries. Sweden is historically the largest investor with 20% – 30% of FDI.[[189]](#cite_note-193) FDI into Lithuania spiked in 2017, reaching its highest ever recorded number of greenfield investment projects. In 2017, Lithuania was third, after [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") and [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") by the average job value of investment projects.[[190]](#cite_note-194)
The US was the leading source country in 2017, 25% of total FDI.[[191]](#cite_note-195) Based on the Eurostat's data, in 2017 the value of exports recorded the most rapid growth of Baltic states and across Europe at 17%.[[192]](#cite_note-196)

Between 2004 and 2016, one of five Lithuanians emigrated, seeking better opportunities and higher salaries abroad.[[193]](#cite_note-197) Long term emigration and economic growth has resulted in a shortage in the labor market[[194]](#cite_note-198) and growth in salaries being larger than growth in labor efficiency.[[195]](#cite_note-archive_org_11-199) Unemployment in 2017 was 8%.[[196]](#cite_note-200) As of 2022, median wealth per adult was [$](/wiki/USD "USD")32,000 (mean was $70,000), while total national wealth was $147 billion.[[197]](#cite_note-CS2023-1-201) As of 2023 Q2, the average monthly gross salary was €2,000.[[198]](#cite_note-https://osp.stat.gov.lt-202) Implicit tax rates on labor, capital and corporate income in Lithuania have been among of the lowest in the EU.[[199]](#cite_note-epp.eurostat.ec.europa.eu-203) The nominal personal income tax rate is 20%, with 32% applied on high incomes. The corporate tax rate is 16% for most companies and 6% for small businesses; 7 [free economic zones](/wiki/Free_economic_zone "Free economic zone") operate.[[200]](#cite_note-204)

[Information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology") production is growing, reaching €2 billion in 2016.[[201]](#cite_note-205) In 2017, 35[[202]](#cite_note-206) [financial technology](/wiki/Financial_technology "Financial technology") companies came to Lithuania as a result of the government and [Bank of Lithuania](/wiki/Bank_of_Lithuania "Bank of Lithuania") simplifying procedures.[[203]](#cite_note-207) Lithuania has granted a total of 39 e-money licenses, second in the EU to the UK. In 2018, [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") set up a payment company in Lithuania.[[204]](#cite_note-208) Europe's first international [blockchain](/wiki/Blockchain "Blockchain") centre launched in Vilnius in 2018.[[205]](#cite_note-209) Since 2021, Lithuania has issued hundreds of licenses for cryptocurrency exchange and storage operations, making it one of the leading countries in the EU in this sector.[[206]](#cite_note-210)

### Agriculture

Main article: [Agriculture in Lithuania](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Lithuania "Agriculture in Lithuania")

Agriculture has been one of Lithuania's most important occupations for many centuries.[[207]](#cite_note-211) Accession to the European Union in 2004 ushered in a new agricultural era. The EU pursues a very high standard of food safety and purity. In 1999, the Seimas adopted a law on product safety, and in 2000 it adopted a law on food.[[208]](#cite_note-212)[[209]](#cite_note-213) The reform of the agricultural market has been carried out on the basis of these two laws.

In 2016, agricultural production was €2.3 billion. [Cereal crops](/wiki/Cereal_crops "Cereal crops") occupied the largest part (5710 tons); other significant types include: [sugar beet](/wiki/Sugar_beet "Sugar beet") (934 tons), [rapeseed](/wiki/Rapeseed "Rapeseed") (393 tons) and [potatoes](/wiki/Potato "Potato") (340 tons). Products totaling €4,385 million were exported to foreign markets, of which products for €3,165 million were of Lithuanian origin. Export of agricultural and food products accounted for 19% of all exports of goods.[[210]](#cite_note-214)

[Organic farming](/wiki/Organic_farming "Organic farming") is becoming more popular. The status of organic growers and producers is granted by the public body *Ekoagros*. In 2016, there were 2539 such farms that occupied 225,542 hectares. Of these, 43% were cereals, 31% perennial grasses, 14% leguminous crops and 12% others.[[211]](#cite_note-215)

### Science and technology

Lithuania ranks moderately in the [International Innovation Index](/wiki/International_Innovation_Index "International Innovation Index"),[[212]](#cite_note-216) and is placed 15th among EU countries by the [European Innovation Scoreboard](/wiki/European_Innovation_Scoreboard "European Innovation Scoreboard").[[213]](#cite_note-217) Lithuania was ranked 33th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[214]](#cite_note-218)[[215]](#cite_note-219) Lasers and [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology") are flagship fields of the science and high-tech industry.[[216]](#cite_note-220)[[217]](#cite_note-221) *Šviesos konversija* ("Light Conversion") has developed a [femtosecond](/wiki/Femtosecond "Femtosecond") laser system that has 80% market share worldwide, with applications in DNA research, ophthalmological surgeries, and nanotechnology.[[218]](#cite_note-222)[[219]](#cite_note-223) The [Vilnius University](/wiki/Vilnius_University "Vilnius University") Laser Research Center has developed one of the most powerful femtosecond lasers in the world dedicated primarily to [oncological diseases](/wiki/Oncology "Oncology").[[220]](#cite_note-224) In 1963, [Vytautas Straižys](/wiki/Vytautas_Strai%C5%BEys "Vytautas Straižys") and his colleagues created [Vilnius photometric system](/wiki/Vilnius_photometric_system "Vilnius photometric system") that is used in astronomy.[[221]](#cite_note-225) Noninvasive intracranial pressure and blood flow measuring devices were developed by [Kaunas University of Technology](/wiki/Kaunas_University_of_Technology "Kaunas University of Technology") scientist A. Ragauskas.[[222]](#cite_note-226) Kęstutis Pyragas contributed to the study of [chaos theory](/wiki/Control_of_chaos "Control of chaos") with his method of delayed feedback control, the [Pyragas method](/wiki/Pyragas_method "Pyragas method"). [Kavli Prize](/wiki/Kavli_Prize "Kavli Prize") laureate [Virginijus Šikšnys](/wiki/Virginijus_%C5%A0ik%C5%A1nys "Virginijus Šikšnys") is known for his discoveries in [CRISPR](/wiki/CRISPR "CRISPR"), namely with respect to CRISPR-[Cas9](/wiki/Cas9 "Cas9").[[223]](#cite_note-227)[[224]](#cite_note-228)

Lithuania has launched three satellites to space: [LitSat-1](/wiki/LitSat-1 "LitSat-1"), [Lituanica SAT-1](/wiki/Lituanica_SAT-1 "Lituanica SAT-1") and [LituanicaSAT-2](/wiki/LituanicaSAT-2 "LituanicaSAT-2").[[225]](#cite_note-229) [Lithuanian Museum of Ethnocosmology](/wiki/Lithuanian_Museum_of_Ethnocosmology "Lithuanian Museum of Ethnocosmology") and [Molėtai Astronomical Observatory](/wiki/Mol%C4%97tai_Astronomical_Observatory "Molėtai Astronomical Observatory") is located in [Kulionys](/wiki/Kulionys "Kulionys").[[226]](#cite_note-230) Fifteen R&D institutions are members of [Lithuanian Space Association](/wiki/Lithuanian_Space_Association "Lithuanian Space Association"); Lithuania is a cooperating state with [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency").[[227]](#cite_note-231)[[228]](#cite_note-232) [Rimantas Stankevičius](/wiki/Rimantas_Stankevi%C4%8Dius "Rimantas Stankevičius") is the only ethnically Lithuanian astronaut.[[229]](#cite_note-233)

Lithuania in 2018 became an Associated Member State of [CERN](/wiki/CERN "CERN").[[230]](#cite_note-234) Two CERN incubators in Vilnius and Kaunas will be hosted.[[231]](#cite_note-235) The most advanced scientific research is being conducted at the Life Sciences Center,[[232]](#cite_note-236) Center For Physical Sciences and Technology.[[233]](#cite_note-237) From 2011 to 2016, yearly growth of the biotech and life science sector reached 22%. 16 academic institutions, 15 R&D centres (science parks and innovation valleys) and more than 370 manufacturers operate in the life science and biotech industry.[[234]](#cite_note-238)

In 2008 the Valley development programme was started aiming to upgrade scientific research infrastructure and encourage business and science cooperation. Five R&D Valleys were launched – Jūrinis (maritime technologies), Nemunas (agro, bioenergy, forestry), Saulėtekis (laser and light, semiconductor), Santara (biotechnology, medicine), Santaka (sustainable chemistry and pharmacy).[[235]](#cite_note-239) Lithuanian Innovation Center was created to provide support for innovations and research institutions.[[236]](#cite_note-240)

[Druskininkai](/wiki/Druskininkai "Druskininkai") is a popular [spa town](/wiki/Spa_town "Spa town").

According to the [World Travel and Tourism Council](/wiki/World_Travel_and_Tourism_Council "World Travel and Tourism Council"), the tourism sector contributed approximately €1.7 billion to the national economy in 2023, accounting for 2.3% of the country's GDP. This represents a notable recovery from the downturn caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, although it remains below pre-pandemic levels.[[237]](#cite_note-241)[[238]](#cite_note-242) In 2023, Lithuania welcomed over 1.1 million international visitors, marking a substantial recovery from the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"). Foreign tourists accounted for nearly a third of the total population. The majority of international tourists originated from neighboring countries, notably Belarus and Poland.[[239]](#cite_note-:2-243) [Domestic tourism](/wiki/Domestic_tourism "Domestic tourism") has also seen significant growth, with residents making around 12.8 million trips within the country in 2023. Most of these were same-day trips, totaling nearly 10 million.[[239]](#cite_note-:2-243)

Key urban centres such as Vilnius, Kaunas, and Klaipėda attract numerous visitors because of their rich history and cultural offerings. Vilnius is renowned for its [baroque architecture](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") and has been recognized as the [European Green Capital](/wiki/European_Green_Capital_Award "European Green Capital Award") for 2025.[[240]](#cite_note-244) Seaside resorts like [Palanga](/wiki/Palanga "Palanga") and [Neringa](/wiki/Neringa_Municipality "Neringa Municipality") are popular for their sandy beaches and unique landscapes. Neringa, part of the Curonian Spit, is [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") known for its colossal sand dunes and pine forests.[[241]](#cite_note-:3-245) Spa towns such as [Druskininkai](/wiki/Druskininkai "Druskininkai") offer wellness retreats, with Druskininkai featuring the largest water park in Eastern Europe and the [Snow Arena](/wiki/Snow_Arena "Snow Arena"), one of the largest indoor skiing slopes in Europe.[[242]](#cite_note-246)

Lithuania offers diverse outdoor activities. Hot air ballooning is particularly popular in Vilnius and [Trakai](/wiki/Trakai "Trakai"), providing aerial views of the historic landscapes. Cycling tourism is on the rise, with the country boasting over 3,769 km of bicycle tracks, including 1,988 km of asphalt pavement. The EuroVelo 10 (Baltic Sea Cycle Route) traverses the Curonian Spit, offering views of sand dunes and coastal towns like Palanga and [Nida](/wiki/Nida,_Lithuania "Nida, Lithuania").[[241]](#cite_note-:3-245) The EuroVelo 11 (East Europe Route) passes through eastern Lithuania, including Vilnius and Trakai, and natural areas like [Labanoras Regional Park](/wiki/Labanoras_Regional_Park "Labanoras Regional Park").[[243]](#cite_note-247) The [Nemunas Delta Regional Park](/wiki/Nemunas_Delta_Regional_Park "Nemunas Delta Regional Park") is a haven for birdwatchers, hosting over 300 bird species, including rare and endangered ones like the [greater spotted eagle](/wiki/Greater_spotted_eagle "Greater spotted eagle") and [aquatic warbler](/wiki/Aquatic_warbler "Aquatic warbler").[[244]](#cite_note-248) The park's wetlands serve as crucial resting sites for migratory birds.[[245]](#cite_note-249)

## Communication

Main article: [Telecommunications in Lithuania](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_Lithuania "Telecommunications in Lithuania")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(August 2025)* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/44/Bures_business_centre_in_Vilnius_%282016%29.jpg/250px-Bures_business_centre_in_Vilnius_%282016%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bures_business_centre_in_Vilnius_(2016).jpg)

[Telia](/wiki/Telia_Company "Telia Company") (skyscraper with the old [Teo LT](/wiki/Teo_LT "Teo LT") logo) and [Huawei](/wiki/Huawei "Huawei") headquarters in [Vilnius](/wiki/Vilnius "Vilnius")

Lithuania has a well developed communications infrastructure. The country has 2.8 million citizens[[246]](#cite_note-250) and 5 million SIM cards.[[247]](#cite_note-251) The largest LTE (4G) mobile network covers 97% of Lithuania's territory.[[248]](#cite_note-252) Usage of fixed phone lines has been rapidly decreasing due to rapid expansion of mobile-cellular services.[[249]](#cite_note-253) In 2017, Lithuania was top 30 in the world by average mobile broadband speeds and top 20 by average fixed broadband speeds.[[250]](#cite_note-254)
Lithuania was also top 7 in 2017 in the [List of countries by 4G LTE penetration](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_4G_LTE_penetration "List of countries by 4G LTE penetration"). In 2016, Lithuania was ranked 17th in United Nations' e-participation index.[[251]](#cite_note-255)[[252]](#cite_note-256) There are four TIER III datacenters in Lithuania.[[253]](#cite_note-257)
Lithuania is 44th globally ranked country on data center density according to Cloudscene.[[254]](#cite_note-258)

Development of Rural Areas Broadband Network (RAIN) was started with the objective to provide residents, state and municipal authorities and businesses with fibre-optic broadband access in rural areas. RAIN infrastructure allows 51 communications operators to provide network services to their clients. The project was funded by the European Union and the Lithuanian government.[[255]](#cite_note-259)[[256]](#cite_note-260) 72% of households have access to internet, a number which in 2017 was among EU's lowest[[257]](#cite_note-261) and in 2016 ranked 97th by [CIA World Factbook](/wiki/CIA_World_Factbook "CIA World Factbook").[[258]](#cite_note-262) The number of households with internet access is expected to increase and reach 77% by 2021.[[259]](#cite_note-263) Almost 50% of Lithuanians had smartphones in 2016, a number that is expected to increase to 65% by 2022.[[260]](#cite_note-264)
Lithuania has the highest FTTH (Fiber to the home) penetration rate in Europe (36.8% in September 2016) according to FTTH Council Europe.[[261]](#cite_note-265)

## Infrastructure

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Lithuania](/wiki/Transport_in_Lithuania "Transport in Lithuania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Lithuania-roads-%28E%29.png/250px-Lithuania-roads-%28E%29.png)](/wiki/File:Lithuania-roads-(E).png)

Major highways in Lithuania

[Rail transport](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Lithuania "Rail transport in Lithuania") consists of 1,762 km (1,095 mi) of 1,520 mm (4 ft 11.8 in) [Russian gauge](/wiki/Russian_gauge "Russian gauge") railway, of which 122 km (76 mi) are electrified, and 115 km (71 mi) of European [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard-gauge_railway "Standard-gauge railway") lines starting at the [Lithuania–Poland border](/wiki/Lithuania%E2%80%93Poland_border "Lithuania–Poland border").[[262]](#cite_note-266) Lithuania received its first railway connection in the middle of the 19th century, when the [Saint Petersburg–Warsaw railway](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg%E2%80%93Warsaw_railway "Saint Petersburg–Warsaw railway") was constructed. It included a stretch from [Daugavpils](/wiki/Daugavpils "Daugavpils") via Vilnius and Kaunas to Virbalis. The first and only still operating [Kaunas tunnel](/wiki/Kaunas_Railway_Tunnel "Kaunas Railway Tunnel") was completed in 1860. The [Rail Baltica](/wiki/Rail_Baltica "Rail Baltica") railway will link Kaunas to Finland, Estonia, Latvia, Poland, and Germany.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/MariampoleN%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD.JPG/250px-MariampoleN%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD.JPG)](/wiki/File:MariampoleN%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD.JPG)

[Marijampolė railway station](/wiki/Marijampol%C4%97_railway_station "Marijampolė railway station"), completed in 1924

Among EU countries, Lithuania has the highest share of freight transported by rail, at 31.7% in 2023.[[263]](#cite_note-267) This has dropped over 50% since 2018, caused mainly by [sanctions imposed on Belarusian exports](/wiki/Economy_of_Belarus#Sanctions "Economy of Belarus") and [Russia](/wiki/International_sanctions_during_the_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "International sanctions during the Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[264]](#cite_note-268)[[265]](#cite_note-269) In 2017, [Lithuanian Railways](/wiki/Lithuanian_Railways "Lithuanian Railways") (*Lietuvos Geležinkeliai*), the company that operates most of the railway lines, received an EU penalty for breaching the EU's antitrust laws and restricting competition in rail freight.[[266]](#cite_note-270)

Lithuania has an extensive network of motorways. WEF grades Lithuanian roads at 4.7 / 7.0[[267]](#cite_note-271) and the Lithuanian road authority (LAKD) at 6.5 / 10.0.[[268]](#cite_note-272) Lithuanian trucking companies drew attention in 2016[[269]](#cite_note-273) and 2017[[270]](#cite_note-274) with huge and record-breaking orders of trucks. Almost 90% of commercial truck traffic is international transport, the highest of any EU country.[[271]](#cite_note-275) Transportation is the third largest sector of the Lithuanian economy.[[272]](#cite_note-276) The sector accounts for 40% of national energy consumption and 75% of oil usage. The car fleet is among the oldest within the European Union and constitutes the most significant single source of domestic [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions").[[273]](#cite_note-277)

The [Port of Klaipėda](/wiki/Port_of_Klaip%C4%97da "Port of Klaipėda") is the only commercial cargo port in Lithuania. In 2011 45.5 million tons of cargo were handled (including [Būtingė oil terminal](/wiki/B%C5%ABting%C4%97_oil_terminal "Būtingė oil terminal") figures).[[274]](#cite_note-278) The port is not among the EU's 20 largest ports,[[275]](#cite_note-279)[[276]](#cite_note-280) but it is the eighth largest port in the Baltic Sea region[[277]](#cite_note-281)[[278]](#cite_note-282) with ongoing expansion plans.[[279]](#cite_note-283) As of 2022, the LIWA (Lithuanian Inland Waterways Authority, *Vidaus vandens keliu direkcija* in Lithuanian) is developing a strategy to resurrect cargo shipping on the Nemunas. Its fleet of electric ships will travel 260 km between the Port of Klaipda and the industrial and transportation centre of Kaunas.[[280]](#cite_note-:159-284) The project is anticipated to need a €75.7 million initial investment, and estimated to eliminate 48,000 truck trips annually.[[281]](#cite_note-285)[[282]](#cite_note-286) The inland river cargo port in Marvelė, linking Kaunas and Klaipėda, received its first cargo in 2019.[[283]](#cite_note-287)

[Vilnius Airport](/wiki/Vilnius_Airport "Vilnius Airport") is the largest airport in Lithuania and 91st [busiest airport in Europe](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Europe "List of the busiest airports in Europe"). It served 3.8 million passengers in 2016.[[284]](#cite_note-288) Other airports include [Kaunas Airport](/wiki/Kaunas_Airport "Kaunas Airport"), [Palanga International Airport](/wiki/Palanga_International_Airport "Palanga International Airport") and [Šiauliai Airport](/wiki/%C5%A0iauliai_International_Airport "Šiauliai International Airport"). Kaunas Airport is a small commercial cargo airport that started regular commercial cargo traffic in 2011.[[285]](#cite_note-289)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Lithuania](/wiki/Energy_in_Lithuania "Energy in Lithuania")

See also: [Renewable energy in Lithuania](/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_Lithuania "Renewable energy in Lithuania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/FSRU_Independence_in_the_port_of_Klaip%C4%97da%2C_Lithuania.jpg/250px-FSRU_Independence_in_the_port_of_Klaip%C4%97da%2C_Lithuania.jpg)](/wiki/File:FSRU_Independence_in_the_port_of_Klaip%C4%97da,_Lithuania.jpg)

[FSRU *Independence*](/wiki/FSRU_Independence "FSRU Independence") in port of Klaipėda

Systematic diversification of energy imports and resources is the key energy strategy.[[286]](#cite_note-290) Long-term aims were defined in National Energy Independence strategy in 2012.[[287]](#cite_note-291) It was estimated that strategic energy independence initiatives will cost €6.3–7.8 billion and provide annual savings of €0.9–1.1 billion.

After the decommissioning of the [Ignalina Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Ignalina_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Ignalina Nuclear Power Plant"), Lithuania turned from electricity exporter to electricity importer. As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Lithuania&action=edit), 66% of electrical power was imported.[[288]](#cite_note-292) Unit No. 1 was closed in 2004, as a condition of entry into the European Union; Unit No. 2 was closed down in 2009. Proposals have been made to construct a new [Visaginas Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Visaginas_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Visaginas Nuclear Power Plant").[[289]](#cite_note-293) However, [a non-binding referendum](/wiki/2012_Lithuanian_nuclear_power_referendum "2012 Lithuanian nuclear power referendum") held in 2012 clouded the prospects for the Visaginas project, as 63% of voters said no to a new nuclear power plant.[[290]](#cite_note-294)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Kruonis_Pumped_Storage_Plant.Lithuania.jpg/250px-Kruonis_Pumped_Storage_Plant.Lithuania.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kruonis_Pumped_Storage_Plant.Lithuania.jpg)

[Kruonis Pumped Storage Plant](/wiki/Kruonis_Pumped_Storage_Plant "Kruonis Pumped Storage Plant")

The main source of electrical power is the [Elektrėnai Power Plant](/wiki/Elektr%C4%97nai_Power_Plant "Elektrėnai Power Plant"). Other primary sources are the [Kruonis Pumped Storage Plant](/wiki/Kruonis_Pumped_Storage_Plant "Kruonis Pumped Storage Plant") and [Kaunas Hydroelectric Power Plant](/wiki/Kaunas_Hydroelectric_Power_Plant "Kaunas Hydroelectric Power Plant"). Kruonis Pumped Storage Plant is the only power plant in the Baltic states to be used for regulation of the power system's operation with generating capacity of 900 MW for at least 12 hours.[[291]](#cite_note-295) First geothermal heating plant ([Klaipėda Geothermal Demonstration Plant](/wiki/Klaip%C4%97da_Geothermal_Demonstration_Plant "Klaipėda Geothermal Demonstration Plant")) in the Baltic Sea region was built in 2004.

Lithuania–Sweden submarine electricity interconnection [NordBalt](/wiki/NordBalt "NordBalt") and Lithuania–Poland electricity interconnection [LitPol Link](/wiki/LitPol_Link "LitPol Link") were launched at the end of 2015.[[292]](#cite_note-296) In 2018, synchronising the Baltic states' electricity grid with the [Synchronous grid of Continental Europe](/wiki/Synchronous_grid_of_Continental_Europe "Synchronous grid of Continental Europe") has started.[[293]](#cite_note-297) In 2016, 20.8% of electricity consumed in Lithuania came from renewable sources.[[294]](#cite_note-298)

In order to break down [Gazprom](/wiki/Gazprom "Gazprom")'s natural gas monopoly[[295]](#cite_note-299)[[296]](#cite_note-300) the first large scale LNG import terminal ([Klaipėda LNG FSRU](/wiki/Klaip%C4%97da_LNG_FSRU "Klaipėda LNG FSRU")) in the Baltic region was built in the port of Klaipėda in 2014. The Klaipėda LNG terminal was called Independence, thus emphasising the aim to diversify the energy market of Lithuania. Norwegian company [Equinor](/wiki/Equinor "Equinor") supplies 540 million cubic metres (19 billion cubic feet) of natural gas annually from 2015 until 2020.[[297]](#cite_note-301) The terminal is able to meet 100 percent of demand, and is projected to meet 90 percent of the demand in Latvia and Estonia.[[298]](#cite_note-302) [Gas Interconnection Poland–Lithuania](/wiki/Gas_Interconnection_Poland%E2%80%93Lithuania "Gas Interconnection Poland–Lithuania"), also known as Lithuania–Poland pipeline, became operational in 2022.

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Lithuania](/wiki/Demographics_of_Lithuania "Demographics of Lithuania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Seni%C5%ABnij%C5%B3_tankumas.png/330px-Seni%C5%ABnij%C5%B3_tankumas.png)](/wiki/File:Seni%C5%ABnij%C5%B3_tankumas.png)

Population density in Lithuania by elderships (administrative units)

Since the Neolithic period, the [demographics of Lithuania](/wiki/Demographics_of_Lithuania "Demographics of Lithuania") have stayed fairly homogenous. There is a high probability that the inhabitants of present-day Lithuania have similar genetic compositions to their ancestors,[[299]](#cite_note-303)[[300]](#cite_note-304)[[301]](#cite_note-305) although without being actually isolated from them.[[302]](#cite_note-306) The population has little apparent genetic differences among ethnic subgroups.[[303]](#cite_note-307) A 2004 analysis of [MtDNA](/wiki/MtDNA "MtDNA") revealed that Lithuanians are genetically close to the [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages") and [Finno-Ugric](/wiki/Finno-Ugric_languages "Finno-Ugric languages") speaking populations of northern and eastern Europe. [Y-chromosome](/wiki/Y_chromosome "Y chromosome") SNP [haplogroup](/wiki/Haplogroup "Haplogroup") analysis showed Lithuanians to be genetically closest to [Latvians](/wiki/Latvians "Latvians") and [Estonians](/wiki/Estonians "Estonians").[[304]](#cite_note-308)

The median age in 2022 was 44 years (male: 41, female: 47).[[305]](#cite_note-population2022-309) In 2021, the age structure of the population was as follows:

* 0–14 years, 14.86% (male 214,113/female 203,117)
* 15–64 years: 65.19% (male 896,400/female 934,467)
* 65 years and over: 19.95% (male 195,269/female 365,014).[[305]](#cite_note-population2022-309)

Lithuania has a [sub-replacement fertility rate](/wiki/Sub-replacement_fertility_rate "Sub-replacement fertility rate"): the [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") was 1.34 children born per woman in 2021, and the mean age of women at childbirth was 30.3 years. The average age of first childbirth for women was 28.2 years. The [human sex ratio](/wiki/Human_sex_ratio "Human sex ratio") is male leaning for the age categories 15–44, with 1.0352 males for every female.[[305]](#cite_note-population2022-309) As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Lithuania&action=edit), 25.6% of births were to unmarried women. The mean [age at first marriage](/wiki/Age_at_first_marriage "Age at first marriage") in 2021 was 28.3 years for women and 30.5 years for men.[[305]](#cite_note-population2022-309)

### Ethnic groups and languages

Main articles: [Lithuanians](/wiki/Lithuanians "Lithuanians"), [Ethnic minorities in Lithuania](/wiki/Ethnic_minorities_in_Lithuania "Ethnic minorities in Lithuania"), and [Demographics of Lithuania § Languages](/wiki/Demographics_of_Lithuania#Languages "Demographics of Lithuania")

Ethnic Lithuanians make up about 80% of the country's population. In 2024, 82.6% of residents were ethnic Lithuanians. Several sizeable minorities exist, such as [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") (6.3%), [Russians](/wiki/Russians "Russians") (5.0%), [Belarusians](/wiki/Belarusians "Belarusians") (2.1%) and [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") (1.7%).[[307]](#cite_note-TGKT21-311) [Poles in Lithuania](/wiki/Poles_in_Lithuania "Poles in Lithuania") are the largest minority, concentrated in southeast Lithuania (the [Vilnius Region](/wiki/Vilnius_Region "Vilnius Region")), constituting majority in [Šalčininkai](/wiki/%C5%A0al%C4%8Dininkai_District_Municipality "Šalčininkai District Municipality") (76.3%) and [Vilnius District Municipality](/wiki/Vilnius_District_Municipality "Vilnius District Municipality") (46.8%). [Russians in Lithuania](/wiki/Russians_in_Lithuania "Russians in Lithuania") are the second largest minority, concentrated in [Visaginas](/wiki/Visaginas_Municipality "Visaginas Municipality") (47.4%), [Zarasai District Municipality](/wiki/Zarasai_District_Municipality "Zarasai District Municipality") (17.2%) and [Klaipėda](/wiki/Klaip%C4%97da "Klaipėda") (16%).[[307]](#cite_note-TGKT21-311) About 2,250 [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") live in Lithuania, mostly in Vilnius, [Kaunas](/wiki/Kaunas "Kaunas") and [Panevėžys](/wiki/Panev%C4%97%C5%BEys "Panevėžys"); their organizations are supported by the National Minority and Emigration Department.[[308]](#cite_note-312) For centuries, [Tatar](/wiki/Lipka_Tatars "Lipka Tatars") and [Karaite](/wiki/Crimean_Karaites "Crimean Karaites") communities have lived in Lithuania. In 2021, there were around 2,150 registered Tatars and 196 Karaites in the country.[[309]](#cite_note-313)[[310]](#cite_note-314)

The official language is [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_language "Lithuanian language"), but in some areas there is a significant presence of minority languages such as [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), [Belarusian](/wiki/Belarusian_language "Belarusian language") and [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"). The greatest presence of minorities and the use of these languages are in Šalčininkai, Visaginas, and Vilnius District.[[307]](#cite_note-TGKT21-311) In 1941, the Jewish population reached its peak at approximately 250,000 people, making up about 10% of the population. Today, however, it has dwindled to a very small number. [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish") is spoken by members of the tiny remaining [Jewish community](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Lithuania "History of the Jews in Lithuania"). The state laws guarantee education in minority languages, and there are numerous publicly funded schools in the areas populated by minorities, with Polish as the language of instruction being the most widely available.[[311]](#cite_note-315)

According to the survey carried out within the framework of the [Lithuanian census of 2021](/wiki/Lithuanian_census_of_2021 "Lithuanian census of 2021"), 85.33% of the population speaks Lithuanian as their native language, 6.8% are native speakers of Russian and 5.1% of Polish. As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Lithuania&action=edit), 60.6% of residents speak Russian as a foreign language, 31.1% – English, 10.5% – Lithuanian, 8% – German, 7.9% – Polish, 1.9% – French, 2.6% – various others.[[312]](#cite_note-316) Most schools teach English as the first foreign language, but students may also study German, French and Spanish; Russian is available as a second foreign language.[[313]](#cite_note-317) Around 85% of upper secondary school students were studying English in 2022,[[314]](#cite_note-318) and around 80% of people in the 15-19 age group reported knowing English in 2011.[[315]](#cite_note-319)

### Urbanization

See also: [List of cities in Lithuania](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Lithuania "List of cities in Lithuania"), [List of towns in Lithuania](/wiki/List_of_towns_in_Lithuania "List of towns in Lithuania"), and [List of cities in the Baltic states by population](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_the_Baltic_states_by_population "List of cities in the Baltic states by population")

There has been a steady movement of population to the cities since the 1990s, encouraged by the planning of regional centres, such as [Alytus](/wiki/Alytus "Alytus"), [Marijampolė](/wiki/Marijampol%C4%97 "Marijampolė"), [Utena](/wiki/Utena,_Lithuania "Utena, Lithuania"), [Plungė](/wiki/Plung%C4%97 "Plungė"), and [Mažeikiai](/wiki/Ma%C5%BEeikiai "Mažeikiai"). By the early 21st century, about two-thirds of the population lived in urban areas. As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Lithuania&action=edit), 68.19% of the population lives in urban areas.[[305]](#cite_note-population2022-309) [Functional urban areas](/wiki/Functional_urban_area "Functional urban area") include [Vilnius](/wiki/Vilnius "Vilnius") (population 708,203), [Kaunas](/wiki/Kaunas "Kaunas") (population 391,153), and [Panevėžys](/wiki/Panev%C4%97%C5%BEys "Panevėžys") (population 124,526).[[306]](#cite_note-FUA,Euro-310) The fDI of the Financial Times in their research *Cities and Regions of the Future* ranked Vilnius fourth in the mid-sized European cities category in the 2018–19 ranking, second in the 2022–23 ranking, second in 2023 ranking while the city claimed 24th spot in the worldwide overall ranking in 2021–22 and Vilnius county was ranked 10th in the small European regions category in 2018–19, fifth in 2022–23, fifth in 2023 rankings.[[316]](#cite_note-320)[[317]](#cite_note-321)[[318]](#cite_note-322)[[319]](#cite_note-323)
